Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IAI: A Quiet Growth Alternative Beyond Pure Tech
data.description:
The iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF (IAI) is a compelling alternative growth vehicle that has provided excellent risk-adjusted returns in the recent past. My analysis shows that IAI grows in spurts, remains insulated from the tech growth theme, yet delivers stellar growth, and manages drawdowns reasonably well for a growth vehicle.

The core of the portfolio's appeal is its varied exposure to a broader capital markets theme, with a good balance of valuations. The concentration is a concern - investors should watch the Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and S&P Global developments closely. It may not always perform like it has in the past 5 years, but the balanced growth exposure makes it a compelling addition to a growth portfolio. Thematically it should gain from increased investment activity over time.

Methodology and Portfolio

IAI tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Select Investment Services Index - a market cap-weighted portfolio selected from companies classified as Capital Markets as per GICS classification. The offshoot of this classification is that IAI ends up with a portfolio that is a decent mix of investment and advisory firms, trading intermediaries and infrastructure, and data and index services. Unlike what the name suggests, the ETF is not a pure-play broker-dealer or exchange play. In fact, pure exchanges and brokers constitute only about a third of the overall portfolio. This makes the theme broader financial services with a focus on capital markets.

The total portfolio is small - only about 35 stocks. The concentration levels are quite high with the top 3 stocks alone attributing ~40% of the entire portfolio by weight. Banks like Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley are not only present, but dominate the portfolio because of their large market caps. They are also more aligned with investment banking, trading and market making than other banks like Citi and JPM -hence they make the cut in IAI.

Performance Comparisons

As a result of the portfolio composition which is diversified across the broker-dealer and exchanges theme, the return expectations are not market-agnostic completely. A trading platform-broker or an exchange often does well in times of volatility and does not show the same amount of drawdowns when markets are under pressure. That is not something one should expect from IAI. IAI remains very much tied to market fluctuations. The overall returns have comfortably beaten the broader S&P 500 (proxied by SPY below). That, is not a given across regimes and market cycles. In fact, IAI has underperformed SPY particularly in bearish phases. The one-year trailing returns show a consistent period of underperformance in 2021 after the sharp rally post COVID, and even in 2023 when broader markets rebounded after a rate-depressed 2022.

Periods of sustained underperformance should set expectations with investors who may be looking at the larger overall 5-year return metrics (which is overwhelmingly in favor of IAI) or the recent 2024-25 outperformance. IAI grows in spurts but could stay below the broader markets for large periods of time. It may even show deeper corrections than the broader markets than its market-agnostic theme may suggest. In 2022, it stayed under the radar and underperformed SPY for most of the time.

IAI's market-agnostic positioning is therefore not a given. This lack of agnosticism is not only because of businesses like investment banking and wealth management that are still pro-cyclical, but because the expected resilience from exchanges and brokers are also only in toplines - not valuations. That is, while business in Nasdaq or CME (topline revenues) appear unaffected by the 2020 crash or the 2022 downturn, their valuations still remain sensitive to the broader market conditions. In 2022, most of these stocks actually corrected more than the markets.

IAI should therefore be positioned more as a non-tech growth vehicle - a diversification alternative in growth portfolios beyond the NDX. In the growth segment, IAI does really well. The drawdowns are far lower than QQQ (proxy for NDX), while the growth is far superior - all this without much of the tech story and AI tailwinds to back its case.

IAI is definitely the quiet growth performer or has been in the past 5 years. It should remain that in the long-term as long as the investments theme is alive and going stronger, either through the equity markets, or crypto, or investment banking overall. And that looks a well-supported theme conceptually too - even if tech is where the growth. Investments in a thriving tech segment will have to come via IAI. This theme may not be truly market-agnostic in patches but should be agile enough to keep growing in the long term as long as capital markets remain active.

A Compelling Addition to Growth Portfolios

Although thematically IAI makes sense for long term investors, and its past performance is overwhelmingly in favor if it positioned as a growth alternative, there are larger questions to be answered now. Are the valuations already too high, i.e. have markets already valued this niche growth area well? Is now a good time to enter?

On the valuation front, despite its diverse portfolio, where there are pockets of exceedingly high valuations (like in Robinhood or Coinbase), the overall PE ratio is reported as ~27.5x, lower than the S&P 500's ~30x (proxied by valuations reported for IVV). The heavy weights allocated to Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley and S&P Global act as a stabilizing factor in an otherwise volatile and richly valued portfolio.

The higher concentration in the top 3 also mean the portfolio is a tale of two halves. One richly valued, volatile and likely to deliver higher growth in spurts. The other half rides on lower volatility and valuations. What has helped IAI's cause in the past 5 years is that even this GS and MS dominated relatively docile half has delivered stellar returns.

Overall, this is what I expect from IAI going ahead. It may not deliver the stellar returns we saw in the past 5 years where both the speculative half and the large cap half delivered durable returns, but the balance and thematic alignment is worth a Buy in a growth portfolio. The balance comes from the presence of lower valued components that should provide volatility cushioning, while the growth half delivers the outperformance. Even if the next 5 years do not replicate the same level of outperformance (because the lower valued part of the portfolio cannot be expected to outrun the markets so consistently), it will provide decent risk-adjusted returns to challenge a tech-aligned QQQ or similar NDX growth bets.

The best part of including IAI is that it diversifies the growth portfolio beyond tech without taking away the growth potential. Coming to the timing, this is probably not the best time to Buy IAI or growth bets. I see some slowdown in the growth momentum in the upcoming quarters as the markets diverge and digests some of the high valuation localized bubbles. However, IAI is such an interesting proposition, that its growth cannot be tied down to tech valuations or market valuations as a whole. I don't think I will recommend timing IAI entries. Growth investors should accumulate IAI systematically and step up, when there are larger drawdowns.
